items.title: Spotlight on SIG Storage
items.summary:
<p>In our ongoing SIG Spotlight series, we shine a light on the groups that keep the Kubernetes project
moving forward. This time, we catch up with <strong><a href="https://github.com/kubernetes/community/tree/master/sig-storage">SIG
Storage</a></strong>, the group responsible
for per
items.reason: Introduces SIG Storage – a core Kubernetes sub‑project. The wiki should have a dedicated page covering its purpose, members, and recent activities.

items.title: Coding Agent Horror Stories: The 13-Hour AWS Outage
items.summary: In Part 1, we walked through six categories of AI coding agent failures and why they keep happening. The agent runs as you, with your filesystem permissions and your credentials, and nothing sits between the model's decision and the shell's execution. In Part 2, we looked at one specific version of
items.reason: Discusses AI‑driven coding agents and failure modes, a hot topic for DevOps and security sections of the wiki.

items.title: Docker Content Trust: Retirement and Migration Guidance
items.summary: TLDR: Docker Content Trust (DCT) and the Notary v1 service at notary.docker.io are being fully retired (first announced in July of 2025). This blog explains what is changing, who is affected, and how to move to modern alternatives.  Ten years ago, Docker Content Trust (DCT) gave the container ecosys
items.reason: Docker Content Trust retirement impacts container signing workflows; the wiki needs an up‑to‑date guide on migration paths.

items.title: Docker joins the Athena coalition: a cross-industry collaboration for supply chain security
items.summary: The obvious takeaway from 2026's biggest incidents is that attackers are increasingly using AI to move fast. Docker's CISO, Mark Lechner, wrote about this shift and what every engineering team should do now. What worries us is that the bar is about to drop further. For most of the last decade, findi
items.reason: Supply‑chain security coalition announcement is relevant for the security and compliance area of the wiki.

items.title: Docker Hardened Images enhanced vulnerability scanning with Docker and Aikido
items.summary: Aikido now scans Docker Hardened Images (DHI) with built-in VEX support. Vulnerabilities that Docker has verified as non-exploitable drop out of the queue automatically, so developers spend their time on findings that actually matter. This post walks through what changed, why it matters, and how use
items.reason: New vulnerability‑scanning integration (Aikido + VEX) for Docker Hardened Images warrants a how‑to article.

items.title: 5 Software Supply Chain Security Best Practices for Development Teams
items.summary: Understanding software supply chain security is one thing. Putting it into practice across a real pipeline, with real deadlines and real constraints, is another. Most organizations recognize that their software supply chain is a growing attack surface, but translating that awareness into concrete, r
items.reason: A practical checklist of software‑supply‑chain best practices is valuable content for security best‑practice documentation.

items.title: How pull request limits are cutting down the noise
items.summary:
Learn how pull request limits can help manage contribution volume in your repositories, and see what’s next on the roadmap.
The post How pull request limits are cutting down the noise appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Pull‑request limits affect repository management; a wiki page explaining the feature and configuration is needed.

items.title: Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing
items.summary:
How GitHub Copilot is making more of each session go toward useful work, so your credits go further.
The post Getting more from each token: How Copilot improves context handling and model routing appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Copilot context handling improvements are relevant for the AI‑assisted development section of the wiki.

items.title: What are git worktrees, and why should I use them?
items.summary:
Git worktrees have been around since 2015, but it wasn't until recently they became popular. Learn what they are, how to use them, and why you might.
The post What are git worktrees, and why should I use them? appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Git worktrees are a core Git feature; the wiki should contain a tutorial and use‑case guide.

items.title: GitHub Copilot CLI for Beginners: Overview of common slash commands
items.summary:
GitHub Copilot CLI for Beginners: Learn how to use slash commands to control your terminal AI agent.
The post GitHub Copilot CLI for Beginners: Overview of common slash commands appeared first on The GitHub Blog.
items.reason: Copilot CLI slash commands are a new user‑facing capability that merits its own documentation page.

items.title: Accelerating researchers and developers building multilingual AI with a new open dataset
items.summary:
A new repository-level dataset, published on GitHub under CC0-1.0, helps researchers and developers discover multilingual developer content across READMEs, issues, and pull requests.
The post Accelerating researchers and developers building multilingual AI with a new open dataset appeared first on T
items.reason: Multilingual AI dataset hosted on GitHub is relevant for data‑science and AI research resources sections.
